trust_assumption: The kernel trusts the syscall and driver IOCTL boundary to deliver only well-formed arguments that its in-kernel handlers can safely parse on behalf of an unprivileged caller.
violation: A memory-safety bug crossed by crafted syscall or IOCTL input lets userland data steer kernel control flow, collapsing the ring-3 to ring-0 boundary into arbitrary kernel-mode execution.

trust_assumption: Windows trusts that a held access-token handle faithfully represents the identity of the thread's caller, so impersonating it speaks for that principal.
violation: A service account holding SeImpersonate coerces a privileged token onto a handle it controls and impersonates it, wielding SYSTEM's identity without ever authenticating as SYSTEM.

techniques.name: Token impersonation/theft via SeImpersonate (Potato family, named-pipe/RPC coercion)

trust_assumption: UAC trusts that an auto-elevating system binary or elevated COM object only runs at high integrity as the result of a deliberate, consented administrator action.
violation: A medium-integrity process hijacks an auto-elevate path (registry hijack, env var, mock directory, elevated COM moniker) so the OS silently grants high integrity with no consent prompt.

techniques.name: UAC bypass via auto-elevate hijack and elevated COM moniker

trust_assumption: The Service Control Manager trusts an unquoted ImagePath string to resolve unambiguously to the one intended service executable.
violation: Whitespace in the unquoted path lets the SCM try an attacker-writable earlier token (e.g. C:\Program.exe) first, launching planted code under the service's privileged account.

trust_assumption: The SCM trusts that the on-disk binary and the service configuration referenced by a privileged service are owned and writable only by administrators, so their origin is authoritative.
violation: A weak ACL on the service executable, its directory, or its reconfigurable settings lets an unprivileged user replace the binary or repoint the service, and it executes as the privileged account.

techniques.name: Weak service binary/permission and writable service config hijack

trust_assumption: The OS trusts that a setuid-root binary or a sudo rule drops privilege safely, confining the elevated context to exactly the vetted operation the program was meant to perform.
violation: A permissive sudo entry or an exploitable SUID binary lets a user reach a shell, file write, or command path while still holding root, escaping the intended narrow elevation.

techniques.name: SUID binary and sudo rule abuse (GTFOBins escape)

trust_assumption: Linux trusts that a fine-grained file capability granted to a binary confers only the one narrow kernel power it names and nothing that composes back into full root.
violation: A single potent capability (CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_DAC_READ_SEARCH, CAP_SETUID) on a binary is leveraged to read root-owned secrets, mount, or change UID, reconstituting full privilege from a supposedly scoped grant.

techniques.name: Linux capability abuse for privilege escalation

trust_assumption: Each individual IAM role assumption is trusted as an in-scope, intended grant, with no party reasoning about what the transitive chain of assumptions composes to.
violation: An attacker walks a chain of assume-role, PassRole, or policy-edit permissions where every hop is individually authorized but the composition reaches an administrator role the principal was never meant to hold.

techniques.name: Cloud IAM role chaining and policy privilege escalation

trust_assumption: A trusted, signed process is assumed to own and author all code executing within its own address space, so monitors attribute that code's actions to the host process's identity.
violation: An attacker writes and executes code in a legitimate process via remote thread, hollowing, or APC, so malicious actions run under the trusted process token and image name.

techniques.name: Process injection and hollowing (CreateRemoteThread, process hollowing, APC, thread hijack)

trust_assumption: The OS image-load and security telemetry pipeline trusts that any executable module a process runs arrived through the loader from a named file on disk that scanners could inspect.
violation: A PE or DLL is mapped and relocated entirely in memory without touching the loader or disk, so the running module has no backing file and no load event for defenders to scan or attribute.

trust_assumption: The kernel trusts any driver bearing a valid Authenticode signature as authorized to load, treating the signature as proof of safety rather than merely of origin.
violation: An attacker loads a legitimately-signed but vulnerable driver and exploits it as a kernel read/write primitive to disable EDR or run kernel code, riding the signature trust around code integrity.

techniques.name: Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver (BYOVD) to tamper kernel/EDR

trust_assumption: The EDR trusts the integrity of its own in-process userland hooks and the syscall path beneath them to faithfully report every sensitive call a monitored process makes.
violation: An attacker unhooks or patches the EDR's inline hooks, or invokes syscalls directly beneath them, so sensitive operations execute while the sensor reports nothing.

trust_assumption: Application allowlists and behavioral baselines trust signed, native OS binaries as benign by identity, treating their execution as expected administrative activity.
violation: An attacker drives a built-in signed binary's legitimate features to download, execute, or proxy payloads, so malicious behavior inherits the binary's trusted reputation and bypasses the allowlist.

techniques.name: Living off the land via signed system binaries (LOLBAS/GTFOBins)

trust_assumption: Forensic analysis trusts filesystem timestamps as a faithful, attacker-independent record of when an artifact was created or last modified.
violation: An attacker rewrites MACE timestamps to blend a planted artifact into surrounding baseline files, breaking timeline reconstruction and hiding the file from time-windowed triage.

trust_assumption: Detection and incident response trust the local event log and audit trail as a complete, append-only record of activity on the host.
violation: An attacker clears, truncates, or selectively deletes event records, destroying the evidentiary state that monitors and responders rely on as ground truth.

techniques.name: Event log clearing and selective record deletion

trust_assumption: Network defenders trust the visible destination of an encrypted channel (SNI, resolved domain, plaintext DNS) to identify the true endpoint a host is communicating with.
violation: C2 hides resolution inside HTTPS or rides a trusted CDN/SNI while routing to an attacker host, so the observable destination no longer reflects where traffic actually goes.

techniques.name: DNS over HTTPS and domain fronting for C2 evasion
